description: '''Raï''' ({{IPAc-en|r|aɪ|.|i}}, {{IPAc-en|r|aɪ}}; {{langx|ar|راي}}, {{Transliteration|ar|rāʾy}}, {{IPA|ar|raʔi|}}), sometimes written '''rai''', is a form of Algerian [[popular music]] whose origins date back to the 1920s. Younger singers of Raï are called {{lang|ar-Latn|cheb}} ({{Lang|ar|شاب}}) or {{lang|ar-Latn|cheba}} ({{lang|ar|شابة}}), i.e. 'young', as opposed to {{lang|ar-Latn|sheikh}} ({{Lang|ar|شيخ}}, '[[shaykh]]'), i.e. 'old', the name given to older and [[Chaabi (Algeria)|Chaabi]] singers. The tradition arose in the city of [[Oran]], primarily among the poor. Traditionally sung by men, by the end of the 20th century, female singers became common. The earliest lyrics dealt with social issues such as disease and the policing of European colonies that affected native populations.
